topic: How kidney stones form and why they hurt so much
narration: Kidney stones hurt more than childbirth for some people. But why? It starts when minerals in your urine get too concentrated. Think of it like sugar crystallizing in a supersaturated solution. These crystals stick together, forming stones that can grow from a grain of sand to the size of a golf ball. The pain happens when these stones start moving through your ureter, the narrow tube connecting your kidney to your bladder. It's only three millimeters wide. The stone gets stuck, causing intense cramping and stretching. Your body tries to push it through with powerful muscle contractions. That's why the pain comes in waves. Small stones pass on their own, but larger ones may need intervention. Stay hydrated to keep those minerals diluted.

caption:
Ever wondered why kidney stones are considered one of the most painful experiences? 😰

It's all about location and size mismatch. When minerals in your urine become too concentrated, they crystallize and form stones. The real problem? These stones have to pass through your ureter—a tube that's only 3mm wide.

As your body tries to push the stone through with powerful muscle contractions, you experience intense, wave-like pain. The good news? Most small stones pass naturally with proper hydration.

Prevention tip: Drink plenty of water throughout the day to keep those minerals diluted! 💧
